transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hello.
Hello, Aidee.
How are you?
Good.
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you, too.
Thank you so much for, you know, taking the time to chat with us.
What time is it where you are?
It's 4 p.m.
I live in Brazil.
Not too bad.
It's perfect.
Cool.
Well, has, um...
um...
Have you talked, I know you submitted an exercise, so thank you.
It looks like that was great.
Am I the first person you're speaking with?
No, I already had a chat with Matthew two, three days ago.
We already talked to him about workflows and a prompting.
We had a great conversation.
He even shared a few tips with me that I'm already trying out.
So, that we talked about.
And he even showed me a bit of a tool that you're using, how it looks like from inside, workflows, and stuff like this, and some little details of a human input and what is optimized.
So, we had an awesome discussion about it.
Amazing.
Well, I'd be curious to hear, like, how it compares to kind of what you've done in the past.
But before we do that, let me give you my little spiel so you, like, know who you're talking to and what we're talking about.
So, I'm Andy.
I lead a customer operation.
For growthx.
Basically, that means that I think about the high level process of delivering to like delivering what we build to clients.
Not necessarily as I'm definitely not as technical as Matthew.
But I think about more like the client delivery side, the process and operations around that.
And so this role, I think it may have shifted a little bit since you started through the application funnel.
And I think there's two pieces to it, right?

So there is that like expertise in prompting in the workflows and optimizing the processes and making sure that the human in the loop understands like at what point you should intercede, how you make it better.
And really thinks about like the whole of the process and the output and is not just, you know.
Clicking buttons and not thinking about, like, quality and taste in what we're producing.
But also there is, like, somewhat of a project management and account management aspect to it.
So the way that our teams are set up today, we have account directors who are speaking directly with, like, kind of managing the high-level overall strategy of clients.
And kind of the long-term planning and vision of what we're building for clients.
The other side of it is within the delivery team, we have managing editors and the content operation specialists.
I think we're starting to think about turning both of those different roles into one combined role.
But what that means is you would still be interacting with clients a little bit, helping kind of manage the day-to-day of delivery and thinking about...
So...
So...
So, you know, how are we doing against what we said that we would do?
And, you know, what are the blockers?
How do we move projects forward?
So that's where I'll be diving in today.
Since you had a very technical discussion with Matthew, hopefully you'll get a sense of kind of like the two sides of that coin.
Yeah, of course.
Do you have any questions before we jump in?
I think we can jump in.
And I think maybe something will pop up in the process while we're discussing.
So I get in touch on what you're interested in.
So I will get more understanding of what exactly this role applies in terms of communicating with the client and overseeing overall process.
So you can just dive in.
Awesome.
So why don't you start by just telling me a little bit about yourself?
So I'm Yana.
I have a background in journalism, more than eight years of background in journalism.
I started as a journalist and I continue it as a name.
Editor, so I was not only producing the content, I was also overseeing content production and different content strategies in two websites, sportsruing.com.
And in 2023, when AI got public, when ChatGPT became publicly available, I started learning what it is, what is AI about.
I worked in Sportsru at that point, and we started using, leveraging, I would say, AI for our own little ideas, for example, structured news.
It was a very weak ChatGPT in the beginning.
It was not something that we have right now, not any of the tools.
But I got really into AI world.
I really liked the process.
I really got interested in what's inside of this black magic box that does all this interesting thing.
Whatever you ask.
And also at that point, I felt like I had a bit burnt out from journalism itself.
It got a bit narrow for me.
So I shifted to AI.
And I didn't know where to start.
So I started from AI training.
I was an actual trainer.
You probably heard about this.
A lot of people are treating it a side hustle where you train models, you are rating and ranking outputs in their quality.
But I grew quite fast and invisible.
So I was not only an operator.
I was a QA, QA lead.
And I grew to team lead.
I was leading a few processes there.
So if we are talking about client cooperation and deliveries, this was a part of my experience there also.
And after some time, I decided to just mix these two experiences that I already have.
I have editorial experience.
It's a
Journalist, and I have now knowledge and understanding of different LLMs and how they work.
So I jumped into creating the workflows for different websites and different blogs.
It were mostly people who knew me before, and they knew my interest, and they heard about it.
You know, they read somewhere that it's possible, and they're like, hey, do you know how to do it?
Can you help us to stand up?
And they were bringing me with some tasks, asking what exactly they want to achieve, and for what.
And I was building some workflows and systems for them to achieve their goals.
And this is what I've been doing for the last half of a year, I believe.
Together, I was combining a bit with Invisible and working on my free time on doing these little projects in different directions.
So I do believe that I have experience in that side of this job description of.
Connecting with the clients and overseeing the process itself, not only actually manually do it.
So what were you using to build the workflows?
Zapier.
Oh, okay.
So, yeah, just building Zaps.
Okay.
And how – tell me more about, like, this little side hustle that sounds like you've got going on.
And what are, like, the inputs and what do you think is important when clients come to you asking for help?
And what kind of content are you building for them?
So let's start with what type of content.
So just overall, give an understanding.
I had a few projects running.
One of them was restructuring.
So one of the editor offices asked me – they had a work – they wanted to have a workflow of restructuring the news.
So just
For someone posted the news, some popular portal posted the news, but they want to fastly restructure this news on a way how they are doing and there it serves.

So it was about adjusting their style guide, adjusting their understanding on how the news should look like, what should be there, which points are important, which parts are important in the news, and structuring what they have on information from other resource.
to their specific requirements.

So it's working with the style guides, working with a few short prompting, so with their examples, working with their requirements, and in general, the style of what they're using, which elements they're using.
And for example, where is a built-in ads.
It's also important that nothing breaks in the middle, for example, when you put ads there, and stuff like this.'am.'am.'am.
is Holly.
Holly.
Easter.
One of the projects were to create a background for news, also for a different website.
When you see in the news, there is a seller over some kind of a contents information, and we were building the whole system to find this information correctly, to link it to what they already have.
So it looks beautifully and everything.
It's kind of a bit of a SEO structuring to interlink different articles inside of one website.
I also built a fact-checking workflow.
We called it like this, but it's technically a REC process when we were building a workflow to filter factoids that would serve for specific articles.
So, for example, we have a topic for an article, and there was one workflow that we were looking for different articles, different types of articles that are going to be verifiable, that are to be...
Another workflow that would perfectly filter factoids from there that we can use for this article.

That was a very fun experience, especially because LLMs are not really good at reading footnotes, and we were using a lot of scientific articles, and we had this huge challenge of how to teach and how to prompt our models to read footnotes correctly, depending on the type of article and how exactly it's structured.
How did you do that?
It was quite a few, quite a long explanation.
So we had to provide, I think, four examples.
We stopped at the four examples in a prompt.
And we also, it's a thing that you do right now with LLMs that we noticed.
You need to not only explain how to do, but why.
They are getting context more and more.
So we were adding to our prompt explanation why it is important and why it is connected.
So it's not just a number in the end.
It's something that's going to be linked in the end of the article.
And we added a loop of a feedback there.
So another model was checking if this reference is made correctly or not.
That was a really fun one.
And one of the workflows, at this point, already two workflows that we made.
It's creating a content from a zero.
So it's starting with RSS research for keywords.
And then it's splitting for secondary keywords, titles, snippets, full articles on different chunks of text, creating an image, and posting it in a WordPress, posting it in a WordPress.
So this was...
Workflows were made for people who are, let's be direct, they're earning money from this article because there's ads in this article.
So they are looking for the ways how to improve and make it fast.
Because before you were paying someone very cheap to do it, now you can pay tokens and you can pay tasks in Zapier to do it LLM.
It's probably like a highlight.
I had a few more, but I can talk a very long time about this.
But this is the one that I like the most.
That's fascinating.
So it sounds like you have had a couple of clients going at a period of time.
How do you think about prioritization?
I mean, you can use examples from other roles that you've had, but how do you think about when you've got a bunch of deliverables for different clients or...
A bunch of tasks on your to list.
How do you manage that?
I usually start, when I already started taking a client, it's like, they give you a deadline, yes.
My deadline usually, depending on the side of task, but it's at around 20% from the end.
So you have this buffer from there.
I usually structure everything in a Trello so I see what's going on in my calendar.
But when we talk that, for example, there is three deliveries at the same time.
had a situation when I needed to deliver two projects, I think, at the same week.
I usually start with the one that I have a chance to call, not call someone for help.
Okay, I forgot the word today.
To delegate some tasks.
So if I have a process where I can delegate.
I start with delegating some of the parts.
So I know that this is already going.
This is already there.
It's already in progress, and I just need to oversee.
And the project that I need to work closely myself, I will give it more attention because I need to have my hands on the deck and work on it.
So I usually start with it.
And it's like each of days starts with seeing what is going on right now and what is going on with the projects and deliveries.
So I think having this buffer in the end and delegating the parts that are possible to delegate, it helps me with these hard deadlines.
And what if you're kind of responsible for multiple things?
Like how do you think about prioritization from there?
If there isn't an opportunity to delegate, everything's in your plate.
How do you think about that?
I'm probably not going to be.
Very original here, I use the same metrics, the urgency, importancy, and try to set this little task between these four categories, what is the most important, what is the most urgent.

I also prioritize, when I work with a client, I prioritize what this client is asking, like if I have client on a call, like sitting and waiting for my answer, this is where it should be my priority, because there is a person waiting for me to give them an update, or to give them some kind of a timeline, and explain what is going on, because they're paying for this job, they're expecting for me to communicate.
Have you worked with engineering or product teams before?
Yes, yes, I did.
I had an experience in...
A few months ago, when we were working on a project, when the engineering team was building, it was a much harder workflow, much more complex.
This is why we needed a much more professional, it was requiring coding, it was requiring using volume, it was requiring deeper understanding.
So I was working with this team of coders and engineers, so we're working on creating a much deeper workflow, which was like 100 tasks inside one workflow, that was huge.
It was a deep search process, so it was requiring so, many steps.
So yes, I had a beautiful experience.
I think I really like to be in the middle, to be honest, I found myself enjoying this position, when you are between engineering team.

Who are speaking technical language, and sometimes they don't really understand, or they just don't speak the same language as, for example, client, or maybe content creation team, or human reviewers.

And you are here as a translator, as a person who will explain to each other what they want, why they want it, and how, and maybe help both of them to achieve this beautiful consensus where everything is going to work.
So yes, I had this experience in a deep search project when we were, we had a team of human experts that knew how to do tasks, but we wanted to automate this task.
But task is a big mental, mental task of searching for, for an article, searching some specific artifacts in these articles.
It's a hard mental task with a lot of different specification and translate.
Needed on a language of programming in a Python is quite difficult because they're, as a researcher, sometimes they don't understand how exactly they are doing it.
For them, it's natural to do it.

And my goal was to talk to them to see how their process is structured so it's easier to understand for our engineers because they think in a more structured way, so I bring this structure to them so they create their own little magic.
In the technical side.
Amazing.
And so you said that you got a chance to look at our product and the workflows that we're building.
How does it compare to what you've been doing or working in?
It looks bigger, of course.
It's much bigger scale.
And I saw only one workflow.
Matthew showed me one workflow.
But I see that the structure itself is very similar to what I was doing in a smaller scale.
So I was happy to see that the logic that I'm using right now, it's something that can actually scale to such a big project and can be applied.
I really like the part about brief.
I saw that briefs for creating a content are very big.
They have so many interesting details.
And it was interesting to see that a lot of this, it's actually should come from a client.

Yes, that's when we talk to the client and we see what exactly they want to achieve, who are their target audience, what is their pain point, and what exactly is their, they want to achieve with this content.
They want to be either ChatGPT wants to find them, or they want their own client to like them after reading this article.
Yes, it can be different ways.
So it was really nice to see that I'm on the right path.
If this works on such a huge scale, it means I'm going on the right
direction.
I have a right idea here.
Yeah.
And how do you think about content quality at the end of the day?
So like outputs are, you know, AI can get you like 90, 95% of the way there.
How do you think about setting that bar and then getting it the rest of the way over the line?
I don't like doing it in the very end.
I should admit I really like to have key human steps in between because I don't like giving all the way through AI from the beginning to the end and then in the end fix all mess.
So I always have, I always recommend people who are having this full cycle content creation to review what you put in a prompt.
So this was, this supposed to be a human created to review.
An what exactly, how an article should be structured, what you want to see, what you don't want to see, because it's much easier to fix there than a fix in a whole article.
And of course, in the end, I believe that final check, if you do everything right in the first and the second step, on what you call brief, I call it like a prompt, I call it simpler.
If you do everything right in a prompt part, I also like to inject a style guide.
I usually do it in between, but I believe in your system style guide, it's something inside the brief, mostly.
Yeah, it's an artifact ahead of time.
But I usually put like a step with a style guide there.
So, if you do the style guide correctly in an AI language, not in a human language, in an AI language, you'll structurize it correctly, give correct examples.
And then you review an outline that LLM.

creates for you, then final output will barely need your correction and your touching, because it's already going to have a great base on what it's built on, because if you have a great skeleton, meat is going to grow, and maximum that you need, it's a beautiful bow on its head, and it's going to be standing here smiling at you, but if you don't have the skeleton, no matter how well you build meat, everything is going to fall apart.
So I prefer to start with a quality here, so your final output doesn't need a lot of combing in the end.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Okay, well, those are all the questions I had for you.
I think we have five minutes left, so what questions do you have for me?
I actually got interested.
I understand that you have ops managers who are directly contacting with a client, but how do you seek a...
Communication with the client on this role, like which exactly points of communication you would delegate to this role?

Yeah, so we work a lot in Slack, so we have external channels with our clients, and on a weekly basis, we're kind of on Mondays sending them a note that's like, hey, this is like, these are, this is what we've delivered, this is what's left, these are our goals for the week.

And then we also have weekly meetings with the client, where this person will help generate the content, and often just like be sending it, or we work in Airtable, it's like our content OS is what we call it.
And so we'll say, hey, like there's five articles for you to review, or five outlines for you to review in the content OS, we have keywords, like we have article ideas.
So it's kind of just saying like, okay, these are the pieces that we promised, here, here's the deliverable, here's the deliverable.
Our feedback and going back and forth on that.
So it's like the day-to-day of just like status updates, I think, is the way that you think about that kind of communication.
I'm not sure like if I'm in the right position, so ask it.
But is this position that requires communicating with a client in terms of creating this brief that is used?
Like all this, getting this information, asking maybe some questions to the client.
And is it a part of the job or it's already higher pay grade and more higher ups doing this part?
Yeah, I think it's that the brief is we think we consider it a living document.
So it will be created probably by a different person or another team.
And it's up to the director and like also this role to kind of make sure that they're continually asking questions.
second.
second.
right.
Has anything changed?
Is there anything like, you know, did you guys just do a webinar that we should pull in?
Or do you have other, you know, is there a new product that's launching that we should consider?
What else needs to go into this brief?
Because we want it to keep updating.
Okay, I see.
So we can ask these questions.
And the people who are actually point of content on this, they will deliver our questions and bring us an answer that we need to know.
and how to use them.
Yeah.
How would you think that, like, is that what you would want to see?
Or what would you want to see there?
I think it's a good way.
Because we also don't want to overwhelm the client with a bunch of people coming with them with answers and questions.
And, I don't know, some details.
Give me this, give me that.
So it's a good way to generalize this and bring it through one person.
Because I've been working in some projects when you have...
Five, six people who are constantly communicating to the client and they're just getting overwhelmed.
Like, why are asking me all this?
Why can't we just have like one standard place when we discuss stuff or one, two people who are going to bring me those questions because it's getting...
And sometimes it's even overlapping.
The same question, I can come up and someone else will come up and we will go to the same person.
That's confusing for everyone.
No one wants to be in this situation.
So, yes, I think it's a pretty workable system right here.
just understand, Elsa, that I'm from description, at least from a job description of this position.
So sometimes you will have some questions.
You will need some little details to add to make it more effective what you are doing to structure it better or to add this flow or this feeling or this taste to block post that you're creating.
So it's very important.
And if I have time for one last question, I believe that it's probably very generic.
And I asked Marilia about this, but what is a successful person in this position?
Like what do you see this person doing and how this person is doing it?
Yeah, I think this role has like, I mean, it's evolved since you started applying.
So it's good to ask this question.
I think that this person is curious enough to want to troubleshoot when things go wrong and know what makes a good, like good inputs.
So, you know, talking about the point, different points, checkpoints of the human being in the loop, you know, you have to be curious enough to ask like, why?
Why isn't this what I want and how do I improve it?
So I think that level of curiosity and willingness to dive in and try and fix things and also work with engineers, because this product is really still in development.
It went live a month ago, so it's very, very new.
So the willingness to file a ticket when something doesn't work, ask for what you need, because you're going to be the person that's in these workflows every day, in and out.
And so you'll be one of the people that will know, like, this isn't working, this needs help.
And this is how I would change it, or what I need changed.
You have to be able to, like, express that.
So it really is about, like, playing with the inputs and improving the outputs, and through that also, like, informing how we build out and improve the product.
Mm-hmm.
Okay.
And one detail about, also talk to Marilia.
Yeah.
From what I understood, there is more of a communication with a content creation, editing part of a team.

But how much this opposition connected to engineering, for example, to people who are building the platform, maybe who are building some deeper systems, who speak Python, who speak all this magical stuff.
Is it connected somehow or we live in a more of a different world?
Yeah, they are separate organizations within the company.
We have like shared Slack channels, and you'll work in linear to report issues and go back and forth with the engineers.
So not a lot of like team meetings where it's the engineers and the, and the, um, CMS or MEs, um, but more like communicating back and forth through tickets and Slack channels and things like that.
that.
Yeah.
In Yeah.
You
It's good to have a communication system with the tech team, because I had an experience working with a company where we were very separated, and I found it very confusing for everyone.
They don't understand what is wrong on our site, and we can't actually connect to them to give them our input and explain what doesn't work in our site and how they could help us.
So it's great to hear.
Oh, I think that's all for me.
I really enjoyed this conversation.
I hope I gave you everything that you needed from me.
You can find me in LinkedIn, for example, or an email if you have any more questions.
I can provide you with some details about my projects, or maybe some other experience if you're going to be interested in it.
Awesome.
Okay.
Thank you, Yana.
Thank you.
